useCases: Technical calculations for waist-to-hip ratio assessment
Validate theoretical results before applying them
Quick reference in clinical or research projects
explanation:
**Normal reference values in health** come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. They're guidelines; your personal medical history always takes precedence.

## How It's Calculated

Waist-to-hip ratio: Men >0.90 and Women >0.85 = high cardiovascular risk.

This calculator applies the formula with the values you enter, checking typical ranges and alerting you to values outside reasonable limits.

## Normal Reference Value Ranges

| Parameter | Normal Adult Range |
|---|---|
| Blood pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting heart rate | 60–100 bpm |
| Body temperature | 36.1–37.2°C |
| SpO2 | 95–100% |
| Fasting glucose | 70–100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult a specialized healthcare provider. Values are current to 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: What is a waist-to-hip ratio and why does it matter?
faq.a: Your waist-to-hip ratio (WHR) divides waist circumference by hip circumference. It's a key health indicator because fat distribution around the belly correlates more strongly with cardiovascular risk than total weight or BMI alone.

faq.q: How do I accurately measure my waist and hip circumference?
faq.a: Measure your waist at its narrowest point, just above your hip bones, with a soft tape measure. Measure hips at the widest point across your buttocks. Keep the tape snug but not tight. Measure multiple times for accuracy.
Men should aim for ≤0.90, women for ≤0.85. Ratios above these thresholds indicate increased cardiovascular risk and are associated with metabolic syndrome and central obesity.

faq.q: Does waist-to-hip ratio predict health risk better than BMI?
faq.a: Yes. Waist-to-hip ratio is often a better predictor of cardiovascular disease risk than BMI alone, because it measures fat distribution rather than just total weight.

faq.q: How often should I check my waist-to-hip ratio?
faq.a: For general health tracking, measure quarterly or semi-annually. If managing weight or addressing metabolic concerns, monthly measurements help track progress.

faq.q: Are waist-to-hip ratio targets different for men and women?
faq.a: Yes. Women have more hip mass due to pelvic structure and hormones, so the threshold is lower (≤0.85) than for men (≤0.90). This reflects natural physiological differences.

faq.q: What factors affect my waist-to-hip ratio besides weight?
faq.a: Age, genetics, hormones (including menopause), muscle mass, alcohol consumption, stress levels, sleep quality, and medications all influence fat distribution and WHR.

faq.q: When should I see a doctor about my waist-to-hip ratio?
faq.a: Consult a healthcare provider if your WHR consistently exceeds recommended thresholds, if you have symptoms like shortness of breath or fatigue, or if you have a family history of heart disease.

faq.q: Is this calculator reliable for athletes and muscular people?
faq.a: WHR is less reliable for athletes with significant muscle mass. Combining it with body fat percentage assessment provides better context for athletic populations.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
